preamble: The change from voiceless labiodental fricative [f] to palatal glide [j] is among the rarest documented consonant shifts, as it requires simultaneous retraction of place (labiodental → palatal) and weakening of manner (fricative → approximant). The most plausible phonetic pathway is via an intermediate palatal fricative [ç] triggered by a following high front vowel: [f] → [fʲ] → [ç] → [j]. Attestation is marginal; the closest well-documented case is the simplification of English /fj/ clusters in casual speech.

examples.note: In informal and rapid English speech, the initial /fj/ sequence in words like 'few', 'fume', 'fury' can reduce to [j] through cluster simplification. The fricative is absorbed into the following palatal glide. This is a marginal process, not a regular sound change, and is distinct from the full f→j shift documented in no major language family.

sources: Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English. Cambridge University Press.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages. Blackwell.
